coredata.dc:title: Isothermal section at 1000°C of the Nb–Ti–Si system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Honghui
Du, Yong
Chen, Hailin
He, Yuehui
Pan, Zhu
Schuster, J.C.
Wang, Richu
coredata.dc:description: The isothermal section at 1000°C of the Nb–Ti–Si system was constructed using a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach, supplemented with 10 alloys, the compositions of which were selected on the basis of the experimental results from the diffusion couple. Both the diffusion couple specimens and the alloys were examined by means of optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and electron probe microanalysis. No ternary compound was found at this isothermal section. Seven three-phase equilibria were observed [1: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Si, 2: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si+Ti(Nb)5Si4, 3: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Ti(Nb)5Si4, 4: Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Nb(Ti)5Si3, 5: Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3, 6: Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si and 7: Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si+(Nb,Ti,Si)]. The solubilities of Nb in Ti3Si, Ti5Si4 and TiSi2 were determined to be up to 60, 41 and 17.6at% Nb, respectively. The solubilities of Ti in Nb5Si3 and NbSi2 were measured to be about 31 and 13at% Ti, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nb–Ti–Si ternary system
Diffusion couple
Phase equilibrium
EPMA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nb–Ti–Si isothermal section at 1000°C by Zhao et al. [7].
Microstructure of an Nb–Ti–Si couple annealed at 1000°C for 440h: (a) Backscattered electron image; (b) schematic illustration of phase distribution for (a).

Backscattered electron (BSE) images of representative al1oys annealed at 1000°C for 264h (a) alloy 2 Si43.0–Nb45.0–Ti12.0; (b) alloy 5 Si22.0–Nb58.0–Ti20.0; (c) alloy 8 Si27.0–Nb15.0–Ti58.0; (d) alloy 9 Si22.0–Nb18.0–Ti60.0.
Isothermal section at 1000°C of the Nb–Ti–Si system according to the present work.
Tie-line and tie-triangle data on the phase equilibria of the Ni–Ti–Si system at 1000°C obtained with EPMA from diffusion couple (in at%)
Phase equilibrium data for the Nb–Ti–Si system at 1000°C obtained with EPMA from the equilibrated alloys (in at%)

article.ce:title: Isothermal section at 1000°C of the Nb–Ti–Si system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, University of Vienna, A-1090 Wien, Austria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The isothermal section at 1000°C of the Nb–Ti–Si system was constructed using a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach, supplemented with 10 alloys, the compositions of which were selected on the basis of the experimental results from the diffusion couple. Both the diffusion couple specimens and the alloys were examined by means of optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and electron probe microanalysis. No ternary compound was found at this isothermal section. Seven three-phase equilibria were observed [1: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Si, 2: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si+Ti(Nb)5Si4, 3: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Ti(Nb)5Si4, 4: Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Nb(Ti)5Si3, 5: Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3, 6: Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si and 7: Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si+(Nb,Ti,Si)]. The solubilities of Nb in Ti3Si, Ti5Si4 and TiSi2 were determined to be up to 60, 41 and 17.6at% Nb, respectively. The solubilities of Ti in Nb5Si3 and NbSi2 were measured to be about 31 and 13at% Ti, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The Nb–Nb5Si3 composites exhibit excellent high-temperature strength and creep resistance, but have poor high-temperature oxidation resistance and poor room temperature fracture toughness [1–3]. Alloying with Ti and Al can significantly improve the high-temperature oxidation resistance and room temperature fracture toughness of the Nb–Nb5Si3 composites. In order to efficiently develop this class of composites with superior properties, knowledge of the phase equilibria in the Nb–Ti–Si–Al quaternary system is necessary. Because of the complexity of the quaternary system, we must have a good understanding of the phase equilibria of the constituent ternaries prior to pursuing the resolution of the quaternary system. The CALPHAD (CALculation of PHAse Diagrams) method could be employed to calculate the phase equilibria in ternary systems, but the prediction must be justified via experiments.

Among the constituent ternaries of the Nb–Ti–Si–Al quaternary system, the Nb–Ti–Si system is one of the most important ternary systems worthy of detailed investigation. Bewlay et al. [4,5] investigated the ternary Nb–Ti–Si phase equilibria close to the Nb–Ti side using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and electron probe microanalysis (EPMA). The liquidus surface was defined by means of microstructural and micromechanical evidence [4]. The isothermal sections of the metal-rich side at 1500 and 1340°C were established through microstructural and microchemical observations [5]. Liang et al. [6] performed a thermodynamic modeling of the Nb–Ti–Si ternary system mainly by extrapolation from the three boundary systems. Recently, Zhao et al. [7] have constructed the isothermal sections of the Nb–Ti–Si system at 1000, 1150 and 1200°C in terms of the results obtained from diffusion multiples using SEM, EPMA, and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD). The isothermal section at 1000°C measured by Zhao et al. [7] is reproduced in Fig. 1 . As can be seen in Fig. 1, some of the phase equilibrium relationships are presented with uncertainties. Although the diffusion couple technique is a powerful and efficient tool in phase diagram determination, a combination of the diffusion couple method with an investigation of selected equilibrated alloys is desirable in order to increase the reliability and precision of the information obtained about a ternary isotherm [8]. The present work aims at the experimental determination of the Nb–Ti–Si isothermal section at 1000°C by means of a diffusion couple approach with the aid of decisive alloys.

The binary Nb-Si phase diagram was assessed by Schlesinger et al. [9], and thermodynamically evaluated by Fernandes et al. [10]. The Ti–Si phase diagram was reported in [11,12]. The Nb–Ti phase diagram [11] is a simple system without any intermetallic compound. At 1000°C, there are two intermetallic compounds in the Nb–Si binary system, namely Nb5Si3, and NbSi2; five intermetallic compounds in the Ti–Si binary system, namely Ti3Si, TiSi, Ti5Si3, Ti5Si4 and TiSi2.

5mm×5mm×12mm bars of Nb (99.8wt.% purity, Zhuzhou Cemented carbide Works, China) and Ti (99.9wt.% purity, Baoji Titanium Industry Co. Ltd., China), and 5mm×10mm×12mm plates of Si (99.99wt.% purity, Changsha Semiconductor Works, China) were used as the starting materials. The binary Nb–Ti couples were first prepared by diffusion welding the elemental bars in a silica capsule back-filled with high purity argon at 1100°C for 72h. One of the Nb–Ti couples and a Si block were ground, polished, cleaned, and then swathed together with Mo wires to make a Nb–Ti–Si diffusion couple, which was wrapped in Mo foil. Three well-prepared Nb–Ti–Si diffusion couples were sealed in a silica capsule back-filled with high purity argon, and annealed in an L4514-type diffusion furnace (Qingdao Instrument and Equipment Co. Ltd., China) at 1000±1°C for 440h. After completion of annealing, the samples were quenched in water.

After standard metallographic preparation, the Nb–Ti–Si couples were examined first using optical microscopy and then using SEM. Nearly all the phase boundaries could be observed in the backscattered electron image (BSE). The phase equilibria in the Nb–Ti–Si couple were determined by EPMA (JXA-8800R, JEOL, Japan). Subsequently, the compositions of 10 key alloys, as listed in Table 1 , were selected on the basis of the experimental results obtained with EPMA from the Nb–Ti–Si diffusion couple. The alloys were prepared by arc melting pure elements in an arc furnace under argon atmosphere using a non-consumable tungsten electrode. The buttons were remelted 6 times to improve their homogeneity. The samples were annealed in an L4514-type diffusion furnace at 1000±1°C for 264h. After annealing, the samples were quenched in water. The metallographic samples were first examined using optical microscopy, and then analyzed using EPMA.

The microstructure of an Nb–Ti–Si couple annealed at 1000°C for 440h is shown in Fig. 2 . The BSE images of several representative al1oys are presented in Fig. 3 . As can be seen in Fig. 2, the scales of the Ti(Nb)Si2, Ti(Nb)Si, Ti(Nb)5Si4 and NbSi2 phases on the Nb–Ti–Si couple were large enough to perform accurate EPMA measurement of the relevant phase equilibria. But, the scales of the Ti(Nb)5Si3, (Ti,Nb) 3Si and Nb(Ti)5Si3 phases were too small to get reliable data of the phase equilibria. The compositions of the 10 selected alloys were located in these phase fields.

In the following sections, TiSi2 with Nb in solid solution is referred to as Ti(Nb)Si2, NbSi2 with Ti in solid solution is referred to as Nb(Ti)Si2, and similarly the denotation of Ti(Nb)Si, Ti(Nb)5Si4, Ti(Nb)5Si3, Nb(Ti)5Si3 has the same meaning. Ti3Si with Nb in solid solution is referred to as (Ti,Nb)3Si because the Ti3Si and Nb3Si are isomorphous. Similarly Ti with Nb and Si in solid solution is referred to as (Ti,Nb,Si).

The tie-line data and tie-triangle data obtained with EPMA from the Nb–Ti–Si diffusion couple are listed in Table 1. The data obtained from the equilibrated alloys are listed in Table 2 . Using these experimental data, the isothermal section at 1000°C was constructed in Fig. 4 .

The data on the boundary binary systems were in agreement with literature [9,11]. The following 7 three-phase equilibria were observed: Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Si, Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si+Ti(Nb)5Si4, Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Ti(Nb)5Si4, Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Nb(Ti)5Si3, Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3, Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si and Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si+(Nb,Ti,Si), where the tie-triangle compositions corresponding to the three phase equilibria Ti(Nb)Si2 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Si and Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si+Ti(Nb)5Si4 were obtained from the Nb–Ti–Si couple and accurately determined because of the large scale of the relevant phases. The tie-triangle compositions for the Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Nb(Ti)Si2 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 were obtained from alloy 2 (Si43.0-Nb45.0-Ti12.0) and well determined. The solubilities of Nb in Ti3Si, Ti5Si4 and Ti(Nb)Si2 were determined to be up to 60, 41 and 17.6at% Nb, respectively; the solubilities of Ti in Nb5Si3 and NbSi2 about 31 and 13at% Ti, respectively. The solubilities of the third elements in NbSi2, TiSi2, TiSi, and Ti3Si are remarkably higher than the data published by Zhao et al. [7]. Moreover, the phase equilibrium relationships of Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si+Ti(Nb)5Si4 and Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Nb(Ti)Si2 are different from the observed by Zhao et al. [7], i.e., Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si+Nb(Ti)Si2 and Nb(Ti)Si2 +Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Ti(Nb)Si (the micrograph of the Nb–Ti–Si diffusion couple had not been presented by Zhao et al. [7], which included two of the relevant phases, Ti(Nb)Si2 and Ti(Nb)Si as the evidence).

It should be mentioned that the annealing duration of 264h at 1000°C could not be enough to reach equilibria because of the low diffusivity of Nb at low temperatures. For example, the (Ti,Nb,Si) phase was remaining in the alloy 8 (Si27.0–Nb15.0–Ti58.0) after annealing as seen as in Fig. 3(c), and also small amount of Ti(Nb)5Si3 was remaining in the alloy 9 (Si 22.0–Nb18.0–Ti60.0) as seen as in Fig. 3(d).

Bewlay et al. [5] suggested that Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si was in three-phase equilibrium in the temperature range of 1500–1150°C. Zhao et al. [7] revealed the existence of the same three-phase equilibrium relationship at 1000°C, but denoted it with dashed line without explicit experimental data. In the present work, convincing evidence for this phase equilibrium relationship has not been acquired. In the constructed isothermal section at 1000°C, the same three-phase equilibrium relationship was provisionally accepted. We think more work is required to confirm the phase equilibrium relationships for Ti(Nb)5Si4 +Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 and Ti(Nb)5Si3 +Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si, and to accurately determine the tie-triangle data for the two three-phase equilibria and for two additional three-phase equilibria of Nb(Ti)5Si3 +(Ti,Nb)3Si+(Nb,Ti,Si) and Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)Si2 +Ti(Nb)5Si4.
The isothermal section at 1000°C of the Nb–Ti–Si system was constructed using a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach with the aid of 10 key alloys and better determined than before.

No ternary compound was found and seven three-phase equilibria were observed at the isothermal section. At 1000°C, the solubilities of Nb in Ti3Si, Ti5Si4 and TiSi2 were determined to be up to 60, 41 and 17.6at% Nb, respectively. The solubilities of Ti in Nb5Si3 and NbSi2 were measured to be about 31 and 13at% Ti, respectively.
It has been demonstrated that the diffusion couple approach is powerful in constructing ternary phase diagrams, and especially useful in conjunction with the equilibrated alloys method.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from National Advanced Materials Committee of PR China (grant no. 2003AA302520) is greatly acknowledged. One of the authors (Yong Du) acknowledges Furong Chair Professorship Program released by Hunan Province of PR China for financial support. Thanks are also due to researcher Shitong Li for his help in electron probe microanalysis.
